Q1: Honestly, my study routine was chaotic at best. I'm juggling 11 online courses while working and managing family responsibilities at 29, so I couldn't stick to a neat schedule. I'd typically carve out time on weekends and late evenings when my apartment was quiet, often listening to lectures while doing other tasks. I tried to batch my studying—downloading everything I needed and then working through it in intensive sessions. It wasn't sustainable, but it's what I could manage with everything else going on.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what crisis was happening in my life that week. Some weeks I'd be completely absent from coursework because of work deadlines or personal obligations, then I'd have marathon study sessions to catch up. Deadlines absolutely dictated when I engaged—I'd look at my calendar, see something due in three days, and suddenly that course became my entire focus. Regular times were a luxury I couldn't afford.
Q3: Usually it was either panic about an upcoming deadline or a notification that something new had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything critical since my attendance was so spotty. I'd also log in when I had a rare quiet moment and wanted to download materials for later. Honestly, the LMS felt more like a checkpoint than a learning space—I went there to grab what I needed and then studied elsewhere.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became routine for me—it was always reactive rather than habitual. I suppose the closest thing to routine was my Sunday evening check-ins where I'd scan all my courses to see what was coming up. But even that was inconsistent. My routine was more about the actual studying I did offline than any platform engagement.
Q5: The lecture recordings were absolutely essential for me as an auditory learner. I'd download them and listen while commuting, cooking, or exercising—that's how I absorbed most of the content despite my low attendance. Discussion forums were also valuable because my peer support group would point me to the most important threads, saving me time. The video content especially, I logged over 100 minutes watching, but I retained so much more by listening repeatedly offline.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials and supplementary PDFs unless they were directly tied to assignments. With 11 courses, I had to triage ruthlessly—if something wasn't going to be assessed, it got pushed aside. I also delayed discussion posts until I absolutely had to participate, which is why my engagement looks so minimal. Some interactive activities felt like busywork when I was already stretched so thin.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly contributed to my understanding of exam material or helped me complete assignments. I also valued anything my peer support network recommended as essential. If I could see a clear connection between the activity and my grade, I'd prioritize it. Vague 'enrichment' activities? Those got cut first. Time is my scarcest resource.
Q8: Optional activities might as well have been invisible to me most of the time. Required activities got done—eventually—but optional ones felt like a luxury for students with more bandwidth. There was always guilt about skipping them because I genuinely wanted to learn deeply, but survival mode meant focusing only on what was mandatory. The anxiety of having so much to do made optional things feel almost taunting.
Q9: Absolutely—most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download lectures, convert them to audio files, and listen during my commute and while doing chores. I had notebooks full of handwritten notes that I'd review constantly. My peer study group also shared resources through WhatsApp, and we'd discuss concepts over voice messages. The LMS was just the source; the actual learning happened everywhere else.
Q10: My exam score was over 93%, but my LMS activity was minimal—that disconnect tells the whole story. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it five or six times on my phone while going about my day. I'd spend hours discussing concepts with my peer support group over coffee or on calls, none of which showed up anywhere. My best studying happened in my car, on walks, in bed with my notes—nowhere near a login screen.
Q11: Constantly. I'd access materials exactly once to download them, then never touch the LMS version again. I had folders organized by course on my laptop and phone with everything I needed. This was deliberate—I knew my internet time was limited with everything else I had to do, so I made my materials portable. One download, weeks of offline engagement.
Q12: There were definitely weeks when my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't face logging in and seeing how behind I was. Work would get intense, or family obligations would pile up, and the courses became this looming source of dread rather than something I could engage with productively. The irony is I'm highly resilient—I always bounced back—but those dark periods meant my attendance dropped to barely 40%. I'd disengage to cope, then cram to catch up.
Q13: All of those factors affected me constantly. Eleven courses is objectively too many, and I knew it, but I was trying to accelerate my degree while working. Fatigue was constant—I'm 29 with responsibilities that 20-year-old students don't have. When I was exhausted, I'd log in, stare at the screen, maybe download something, and log out without really processing anything. The LMS became a guilt trigger during those times.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere—worrying about work, planning dinner, just zoning out from exhaustion. I'd technically complete the activity, but I wasn't present. Those were the times when I'd have to re-listen to everything later anyway. My task completion rate reflects activity, not absorption.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, which is why my completion rate was only around 65% despite my high exam scores. The anxiety made starting things feel overwhelming—I'd know exactly what I needed to do but feel paralyzed. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow would bring new crises. Important things got done eventually, but 'eventually' often meant scrambling at the last minute or missing deadlines entirely.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to engage with the LMS directly. I'd work backward from due dates, calculating the minimum time needed, and that's when I'd finally log in and submit. Without deadlines, I might never have opened certain modules. It's not ideal, but deadline pressure was the external structure I needed when my internal motivation couldn't overcome my anxiety and exhaustion.
Q17: Feedback was actually really motivating when I received it. Seeing that my exam scores were high despite my chaotic engagement reassured me that my offline study methods were working. But feedback on assignments was sometimes demoralizing because my assignment scores didn't reflect my actual understanding—I just didn't have time to polish them. I learned to separate my self-worth from assignment feedback and focus on what the exams showed I knew.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and overwhelming. Having everything available online meant I could work on my own schedule, which was essential. But the sheer volume of content across 11 courses made it hard to know what to prioritize. I relied heavily on my peer support group to help me navigate what was truly important versus what I could skim. More structured guidance on priorities would have helped enormously.
Q19: The flexibility of online access made engagement possible for someone with my schedule—that was crucial. But the lack of clear hierarchies in the content made it harder. Everything seemed equally important, so I had to guess what to focus on. Also, some courses buried essential information in optional sections, which I'd miss because I was triaging. Clearer signposting of must-know versus nice-to-know would have reduced my anxiety significantly.
Q20: I think that could reflect students who are struggling to understand the material and keep revisiting it without making progress. Or students who are anxious like me—logging in repeatedly to check things, watching videos multiple times, but not feeling confident enough to submit work. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes it means you're spinning your wheels, overwhelmed and unsure how to move forward.
Q21: That's literally my experience. My LMS engagement was low—I barely logged in, my attendance was terrible—but I got a Distinction because I was learning intensively outside the system. I think this pattern reflects students who've figured out how they learn best and don't need the platform to mediate everything. For auditory learners like me, downloaded content and peer discussions can be far more effective than clicking through modules.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and what they clicked on. They can track submission patterns and time spent on the platform. But they completely miss the depth of offline engagement—the hours I spent listening to lectures on repeat, the study sessions with peers, the handwritten notes I reviewed constantly. Logs measure platform interaction, not learning. They're a proxy at best, misleading at worst.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material—questioning it, connecting it to what I already know, discussing it with others, applying it. It's an internal state of curiosity and effort, not a set of behaviors. I can be deeply engaged while walking my dog and thinking through a concept. I can be completely disengaged while staring at a video that's technically playing. Engagement is about mental investment, not clicks.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my peer support group carried me through this experience—the late-night voice messages explaining concepts, the shared notes, the emotional encouragement when my anxiety spiked. It wouldn't show the hours of audio lectures I absorbed during my commute, or the resilience it took to keep going despite feeling constantly overwhelmed. My data looks like a struggling student, but my grades show someone who found a way to learn despite everything working against her.
